caveats: a: No six-digit wage-weighted task study was found; Upbound is a large operator with virtual and financial-health businesses, so its workflow mix may be more digitized than the LMM population.
rho: The implementation range is judgment; the public-company source demonstrates technical feasibility but not LMM deployment rates.
e: NAICS primary activity does not prove every firm is independent, recurring-service-led, or correctly placed in the supplied EBITDA band.
s5: No industry-specific owner-age series or control-sale rate was found; franchise transfers and account or inventory purchases may not represent qualifying control transfers.
q: The filing describes contract structure but does not measure pass-through of automation savings, and regulation can alter both price and product design.
d5: Upbound includes businesses and geographies outside the lens; GMV and same-store sales are not constant-quality industry demand, and a one-year change is not a five-year forecast.

o: The boundary between a rental operator, an embedded fintech platform, and a retailer is evolving; third-party fulfillment can move labor outside the classified firm without eliminating accountable operating work.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Operators own or acquire durable merchandise and earn repeat weekly, biweekly, semimonthly, or monthly rental payments; customers may return merchandise or obtain ownership through renewal or early-purchase options. Revenue depends on approvals, collections, merchandise utilization and loss control, delivery, installation, repair, and resale or rerental.
lens.excluded_activities: Computer and office-equipment leasing classified in 532420, general rental centers classified in 532310, pure retailers and lenders, captive rental units, software-only financing platforms, and operators outside the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms renting consumer electronics and household appliances to external customers, including recurring rental and lease-to-own operators using stores, e-commerce, or retail-partner channels.

caveats: No six-digit wage-weighted task study was found; Upbound is a large operator with virtual and financial-health businesses, so its workflow mix may be more digitized than the LMM population.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task diary covering store associates, account managers, delivery technicians, repair staff, call-center staff, and managers at independent 532210 operators, paired with current automation-use flags, would materially narrow the range.
a.rationale: Current not-yet-automated exposure is concentrated in customer inquiry handling, application review exceptions, collections communications, merchandising, marketing, inventory and route planning, and administrative work. The SEC filing confirms automated decision engines and a meaningful e-commerce channel already exist, so already-digitized approvals are not counted as fresh exposure; delivery, setup, repair, recovery, and merchandise handling remain physical.

d5.bridge: Upbound's 2025 filing measures recent demand at one large, unusually digital lease-to-own company: Acima GMV grew 8.6%, while Rent-A-Center same-store sales fell 2.2% and segment revenue declined. I treat those opposing signals as a centered, wide five-year constant-quality industry range rather than extrapolating either rate. Its scale, national partnerships, and digital mix likely bias upside relative to independent LMM operators, while its store closures may bias the physical-chain result downward.
caveats: Upbound includes businesses and geographies outside the lens; GMV and same-store sales are not constant-quality industry demand, and a one-year change is not a five-year forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year revenue and active-agreement cohorts for a representative panel of 532210 firms, separated into store, online, and embedded retail channels, would replace the single-company proxy.
d5.rationale: Rental demand should persist because customers value access without full upfront purchase, but the current basket faces substitution from lower-cost retail, used goods, conventional credit, BNPL, and software-enabled lease-to-own. The observed company signals are mixed, supporting a flat base and a wide range rather than a directional forecast.

caveats: NAICS primary activity does not prove every firm is independent, recurring-service-led, or correctly placed in the supplied EBITDA band.
e.change_evidence: A record-level SUSB or business-register tabulation linking 532210 firms to rental-versus-sales revenue, external customers, independence, and normalized EBITDA would determine the eligible share.
e.rationale: The Census definition is directly centered on establishments renting consumer electronics and appliances, so most firms counted in the LMM band should sell an eligible repeat external service. The discount allows for mixed retailers, captive units, firms whose economics are primarily financing or merchandise sale, and classification error.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 532210 ($573,044,345) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 532210 ($5,738,672,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3000 (margins.json[532]: ESTIMATE: rental & leasing is asset-heavy with no clean Damodaran bucket (REITs 43.79% overstates operating rental); 30% chosen as a round mid-point before deriving results); revenue/employee $248,470 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 532210); class <5: 242 firms, avg revenue/firm $498,298 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $149,489 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 89 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,149,640 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $344,892 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 44 firms, avg revenue/firm $2,357,545 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $707,264 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 64 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,588,906 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,676,672 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 19 firms, avg revenue/firm $31,262,895 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,378,868 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 8 firms, avg revenue/firm $557,543,625 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $167,263,088 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 83

caveats: The boundary between a rental operator, an embedded fintech platform, and a retailer is evolving; third-party fulfillment can move labor outside the classified firm without eliminating accountable operating work.
o.change_evidence: Channel-level customer journey data showing the share of agreements completed and serviced without operator labor, plus loss, repair, delivery, and compliance ownership by channel, would materially refine the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Applications, payment, selection, and routine support can migrate to self-service, but an accountable rental operator still must acquire and own merchandise, set terms, comply with rental law, bear loss and residual-value risk, and arrange delivery, setup, repair, replacement, recovery, and rerental. Software and partner retailers can reduce the operator footprint without eliminating the operator function.

caveats: The filing describes contract structure but does not measure pass-through of automation savings, and regulation can alter both price and product design.
q.change_evidence: Cohort-level agreement pricing, renewal, early-purchase, return, and contribution-margin data before and after workflow automation at several independent operators would identify how much gross benefit survives repricing and competition.
q.rationale: Frequent renewal payments and posted product-level economics give operators room to retain some implemented cost benefit, especially within existing agreements. Over five years, short customer commitments, early-purchase options, easy termination, promotions, online comparison, retail substitutes, BNPL, and fintech competition push benefits toward customers through pricing and approval policy, limiting durable retention.

caveats: The implementation range is judgment; the public-company source demonstrates technical feasibility but not LMM deployment rates.
rho.change_evidence: Before-and-after workflow and labor-hour data from at least ten independent rental operators deploying AI in collections, customer service, merchandising, or dispatch would establish realized implementation rather than technology availability.
rho.rationale: Five-year implementation is feasible for communications, decision support, scheduling, and back-office work because digital applications, automated decisioning, e-commerce, and electronic payments are already embedded in the model. Adoption is constrained by fragmented legacy systems, consumer-protection and privacy requirements, the need for human exception handling, and the physical last mile.

caveats: No industry-specific owner-age series or control-sale rate was found; franchise transfers and account or inventory purchases may not represent qualifying control transfers.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction extract from BizBuySell, DealStats, PitchBook, or GF Data filtered to verified 532210 control sales, matched to the eligible-firm denominator and supplemented with owner-age data, would materially change this estimate.
s5.rationale: A five-year control-transfer probability near one in five is a bounded judgment for privately owned rental firms: recurring customer accounts and reusable inventory can support transfer, but asset quality, delinquencies, regulation, owner dependence, and concentration in larger chains reduce the pool of clean transactions.

principal_driver: Recurring customer-account work and established digital decisioning create implementable opportunities across service, collections, merchandising, and planning.
principal_weakness: Physical asset operations, regulation, strong financing and retail substitutes, and unobserved LMM transfer rates constrain both implementation and confidence.
